module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So, far we have looked at what is actually Weber's identification of what is the spirit of capitalism, frugality, honesty, time is money, all of those values. And then we have looked at, well this is not a taken for granted thing in pre-capitalist traditional societies in the ancient and the Middle Ages, capitalism had to really go against a lot of traditional ideas to be established, and those ideas were dominating and the people who were the richest held those ideas. So the spirit of capitalism actually came about in some small fringe groups that's where it arose and why? How did these fringe groups, where did they get this idea from? And this is where Weber talks about this reform movement of Protestantism. And what was the ideology there? It was one of extreme asceticism. It was against the enjoyment of possession. So the idea is you restrict consumption, especially of luxuries. It's against any type of irrational use of wealth. In fact, they consider luxuries as the equivalent of some kind of idolatry of the flesh, where instead of God being important, you are thinking that pleasure is important. And so this philosophical change freed the acquisition of wealth from this traditionalist idea against greed, against moneymaking. Instead, it reframed the act of moneymaking as a direct will of God, as a sign of your diligence, your honesty, and your spiritual focus on your calling, on your work. So there was this reframing which happened from moneymaking is greedy, it's bad to moneymaking and diligent work as a calling is actually willed by God. And so instead of this elegance and luxury, the value system is sober, simplicity. And so instead of these opulent courts, rich gold and diamonds of these kings and monarchs and nobility, instead, it valued the clean and solid comfort of the middle class home as an ideal. Now, the pursuit of wealth as an end in itself was still considered highly reprehensible. But the idea was that the attainment of wealth is a sign that you have diligently worked, and so it is a sign of blessings. You have diligently worked in your calling. So this restless, continuous work in your vocation, in your calling was considered the highest proof of your genuine faith. And this is the most powerful lever for the expansion of what Weaver calls the spirit of capitalism. So now, if you limit consumption while increasing human focus and work, what will that mean? Inevitably, you will end up accumulating capital. Now this accumulation of capital again, instead of consuming it, you are reinvesting it into productive, rational activities. And so this will inevitably lead to a great increase in wealth and productivity. In addition, it is not just the capitalists who are having this view, it's that whole society is that whole group of people. So even the workers are conscientious, unusually industrious, who clinging to their work as their life purpose. And so when this happened in these small New England colonies and in some places in Europe. These were places where you had these Protestants, Puritans, Calvinists these were the most jealous of the Protestant sects. So when this happened in those small places, you had this accumulation of capitalism capital, and so you had this flourishing of capitalism. And so now next we will look at how Weber you know, we should not misunderstand Weber. Weber is not saying that this is a great thing that has happened. He is a social theorist, so he's not saying capitalism is good or capitalism is bad. He is looking at both the pros and the cons. And so in the next section we will discuss Weber's views on, okay, so there was all this religious zeal, frugality, honesty, all of that led to accumulation of capital and, you know, productive views of capital and so that led to economic wealth and prosperity. And then it's slowly the system started spreading to the rest of the world. And now Weber looks at what is the consequence of this? How society now degenerates because of this? The disenchantment which he sees in the modern world and that we shall look at in the next clip.
